items.evidence_text:
Client: Oh—wait, I was thinking what I actually see after taxes. Sorry.
Advisor: No worries at all. Before taxes, what’s the closer number for the year?
Client: Before taxes it’s about seventy-seven thousand a year—like $77,050-ish.
Advisor: Perfect, I’m writing down about $77,050 gross annually. And that’s coming from a couple sources, right—rental income and some business income?
Client: Yes. The rental is the big one, and then I have a little business income that comes in quarterly.

Advisor: Okay. Now on the spending side—what do your total monthly expenses run, roughly? Just the household spending, not taxes withheld.
Client: Around four thousand a month.
Advisor: Four thousand even, or closer to like $3,900?
Client: Closer to $3,900 most months. Some months it’s higher if something breaks at the rental.
Advisor: Right, that makes sense. And just to avoid a mix-up—when we say “expenses,” we’re talking your spending outflows. Separate question: do you have any monthly debt payments—mortgage, car loan, credit cards you’re carrying?

Client: Oh—wait, I was thinking what I actually see after taxes. Sorry.
Advisor: No worries at all. Before taxes, what’s the closer number for the year?
Client: Before taxes it’s about seventy-seven thousand a year—like $77,050-ish.
Advisor: Perfect, I’m writing down about $77,050 gross annually. And that’s coming from a couple sources, right—rental income and some business income?
Client: Yes. The rental is the big one, and then I have a little business income that comes in quarterly.

Advisor: Okay. When you say four-fifty, are you including the taxable account and the retirement account and the cash, plus that private investment you mentioned?
Client: Yes—well, I might be forgetting one. There’s a taxable account, an IRA/401k type thing, cash at the bank, and then a private markets thing through the bank.
Advisor: Got it. If we add those buckets, does about $478,900 sound like the right total for investable assets?
Client: Yeah—actually that sounds right. I was low. About $478,900.
Advisor: Okay, thank you. Let me just sanity-check the pieces with you, Linda, so we’re not missing anything. The taxable account—do you recall roughly what that’s at?

Advisor: You’re right—thanks for correcting me. Cash is cash; we’ll just note it as a bank-type bucket around $47,100. And then the private markets investment—about $39,300 through the bank?
Client: Yes, $39,300. That one I really have to look up sometimes.
Advisor: Totally. And then you do have your primary residence as well—separate from investable assets—roughly $125,900 in value?
Client: Yes, about $125,900.
Advisor: Great. Any loans against the house or anything else outstanding?

Client: Oh—wait, I was thinking what I actually see after taxes. Sorry.
Advisor: No worries at all. Before taxes, what’s the closer number for the year?
Client: Before taxes it’s about seventy-seven thousand a year—like $77,050-ish.
Advisor: Perfect, I’m writing down about $77,050 gross annually. And that’s coming from a couple sources, right—rental income and some business income?
Client: Yes. The rental is the big one, and then I have a little business income that comes in quarterly.

Advisor: And country-wise—you’re in the U.S., correct?
Client: Yes, U.S.
Advisor: Great. And the way my firm is set up, we’re a U.S. RIA—registered investment advisor. Does that match what you were expecting when you booked with us?
Client: Yeah, that’s fine. U.S. RIA, basically.
Advisor: Perfect. One sec—just writing that down.

Advisor: Totally fair. When you say 24—are you thinking federal marginal bracket, not like your effective rate after deductions?
Client: Oh. I might be mixing that up. I’m thinking what I “feel” like I pay overall.
Advisor: Yeah, that’s the common mix-up. Marginal bracket is the one that matters for decisions like Roth conversions and realizing gains. Do you recall seeing 32% on your return or tax software?
Client: Yes—32%. That sounds right. I remember seeing 32% last year.
Advisor: Okay, so we’ll treat you as in the 32% bracket for planning, unless your CPA tells us otherwise.

Client: I think it’s like… four-fifty? Maybe four-sixty.
Advisor: Okay. When you say four-fifty, are you including the taxable account and the retirement account and the cash, plus that private investment you mentioned?
Client: Yes—well, I might be forgetting one. There’s a taxable account, an IRA/401k type thing, cash at the bank, and then a private markets thing through the bank.
Advisor: Got it. If we add those buckets, does about $478,900 sound like the right total for investable assets?
Client: Yeah—actually that sounds right. I was low. About $478,900.

Client: Hi Alex. Yeah, thanks. I’m glad we’re doing this—there’s a lot to keep straight now.
Advisor: Totally hear you. Before we get into numbers, tell me a little about you and what you want this plan to do for you.
Client: I’m 67, widowed, and it’s just me managing everything. I want to keep things growing, but I need it to feel… stable month to month.
Advisor: Got it. And going back to that “stable month to month” piece—let’s start with income so we can see what’s reliable versus lumpy. What do you consider your total gross income for the year, roughly?
Client: Umm… I’d say around seventy-five thousand? Maybe a bit more.

Client: Oh—wait, I was thinking what I actually see after taxes. Sorry.
Advisor: No worries at all. Before taxes, what’s the closer number for the year?
Client: Before taxes it’s about seventy-seven thousand a year—like $77,050-ish.
Advisor: Perfect, I’m writing down about $77,050 gross annually. And that’s coming from a couple sources, right—rental income and some business income?
Client: Yes. The rental is the big one, and then I have a little business income that comes in quarterly.

Advisor: Got it. And when you said “no loans,” I just want to separate it cleanly—do you have any mortgage balance at all on the home?
Client: No, no mortgage. It’s paid off.
Advisor: Okay. Any other loans outstanding—car loan, personal loan, anything like that?
Client: No. None.
Advisor: And credit cards—sometimes people say “no loans” but they carry a card balance month to month. Any non-mortgage debt outstanding right now?

Client: Hi Alex. Yeah, thanks. I’m glad we’re doing this—there’s a lot to keep straight now.
Advisor: Totally hear you. Before we get into numbers, tell me a little about you and what you want this plan to do for you.
Client: I’m 67, widowed, and it’s just me managing everything. I want to keep things growing, but I need it to feel… stable month to month.
Advisor: Got it. And going back to that “stable month to month” piece—let’s start with income so we can see what’s reliable versus lumpy. What do you consider your total gross income for the year, roughly?
Client: Umm… I’d say around seventy-five thousand? Maybe a bit more.

Advisor: Thank you. I know that’s not a fun box to check.
Client: Yeah. It’s fine—just… paperwork.
Advisor: I hear you. Now, risk tolerance—earlier you sounded pretty growth-oriented, but with a cash buffer because the rental income can be lumpy. Would you call your overall risk tolerance “growth,” or more moderate?
Client: Growth. I don’t want to be reckless, but yes—growth.
Advisor: Okay, growth. When you say “not reckless,” is that more about not wanting big swings month-to-month, or not wanting to lose principal at all?

Advisor: Got it—so gross is about $77,050 a year from work. Thanks for catching that.
Client: Yeah, I always think in what hits my account.
Advisor: Totally normal. And just to place you in the right bucket—what kind of work is it? Like sales, operations, healthcare—what would you call it?
Client: Sales. I’m in sales.
Advisor: Okay, sales. And you’re the primary person on the plan—just you in the household, right?

Advisor: You’re right—thanks for correcting me. Cash is cash; we’ll just note it as a bank-type bucket around $47,100. And then the private markets investment—about $39,300 through the bank?
Client: Yes, $39,300. That one I really have to look up sometimes.
Advisor: Totally. And then you do have your primary residence as well—separate from investable assets—roughly $125,900 in value?

Advisor: Income first: you’ve got that rental income coming in monthly—about $65,000 a year—and then business income quarterly—about $12,050 annualized. Still right?
Client: Yes, that’s right—rental monthly, and the business one is quarterly.
Advisor: And you’re still employed overall, with gross income around $77,050 for the year, correct?
Client: Yes, roughly $77,050 gross.
Advisor: Great. Assets—tell me if I’m saying these back correctly. You mentioned a taxable account that sits on an insurance platform—value around $280,350.

Client: Right, yeah. I’m employed.
Advisor: Perfect. One more housekeeping thing, Linda—just so I don’t mess up age-based rules later. What’s your date of birth?
Client: March 2nd, 1959.
Advisor: March 2nd… 1959. Thank you. I always ask because I’ve seen people give me the year and then later it’s off by one and it changes everything.
Client: Yeah, totally. And I always have to think for a second—sorry.

Advisor: While we’re here, I want to make sure we’ve touched every moving piece you told me about—income, assets, and insurance—just so nothing gets lost.
Client: Okay.
Advisor: Income first: you’ve got that rental income coming in monthly—about $65,000 a year—and then business income quarterly—about $12,050 annualized. Still right?
Client: Yes, that’s right—rental monthly, and the business one is quarterly.
Advisor: And you’re still employed overall, with gross income around $77,050 for the year, correct?

Client: Yes. The rental is the big one, and then I have a little business income that comes in quarterly.
Advisor: When you say quarterly—like four times a year—does that feel uneven or pretty consistent?
Client: Pretty consistent, just not monthly. It’s not huge.
Advisor: Okay. Now on the spending side—what do your total monthly expenses run, roughly? Just the household spending, not taxes withheld.
Client: Around four thousand a month.

Advisor: Okay, sales. And you’re the primary person on the plan—just you in the household, right?
Client: Yes, just me.
Advisor: Quick admin thing, Linda—sometimes our notes label people as “Client 1” or “Client 2.” Since it’s just you, you’re Client 1, correct?
Client: Yes, I’m Client 1.
Advisor: Perfect. And role-wise, I’m marking you as the primary household member—does that sound right?

Advisor: No worries at all. Before taxes, what’s the closer number for the year?
Client: Before taxes it’s about seventy-seven thousand a year—like $77,050-ish.
